standard_description: Partition a rectangle into rows and columns of same-size squares and count to find the total number of them.
subject: Math

problems.text:
Problem 1
Which of the rectangles on
Template: Compare Rectangles
has the greatest area? Show or explain your thinking.
Problem 2
Area is measured in
square units
. We can cover a shape with unit squares, then count the number of squares that make up a shape to find its area. For example, if a shape is covered by 12 unit squares, it has an area of 12 square units.
a.   Use square tiles to find the area, in square units, of the rectangles in Anchor Task #1.
b.   Build a rectangle with an area of 15 square units.
Problem 3
a.   Use square tiles to find the area of Figures i and ii on
Template: Tile to Find Area
.
b.   Naiche says that figure (ii) has an area of 18 square units because he covered it with 18 square tiles, as shown below.
###IMAGE0###
Do you agree with Naiche? Explain your reasoning.
Problem 4
Use square tiles to find the approximate area of the figure on
Template: Tile to Approximate Area
.

What is the area, in square units, of the rectangle below?
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
Which rectangle has an area of 24 square units?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Myra tiled this figure with unit squares.
###IMAGE1###
What is the area of the figure?

Problem 1
Pre-unit
Partition the rectangle into 4 equal rows and 5 equal columns.
How many small squares are there in the rectangle?
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
Label the following shaded rectangle with the number of rows and columns of unit squares that it consists of. Then find its area.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Label the following shaded rectangle with the number of rows and columns of unit squares that it consists of. Then find its area.
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
What is the area, in square units, of the rectangle below?
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
What is the area, in square units, of the rectangle below?
###IMAGE1###

Label the number of rows and columns of unit squares of the rectangle below. Then find the total area of the rectangle.
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
Using your square tiles, find the area of the following rectangle.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Construct a rectangle whose area is 16 square units. Then show your teacher.

Problem 1
A rectangle has the measurements shown. Select the three ways to calculate the area of the rectangle in square units.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 2
Find the side length of the rectangle below.
###IMAGE1###

Problem 1
Pre-unit
Partition the rectangle into 10 equal squares.
###IMAGE0###

Problem 1
Build a rectangle whose area is 12 square units on top of grid paper. Then trace around the outside of your rectangle. What do you notice about the rectangle you traced on the grid paper? What do you wonder?
Problem 2
a.   Sort the cards (cut out from
Template: Rectangle Sort
) into categories of your choosing. Be prepared to explain your categories.
b.   Create a rectangle that would fit in each group.
###IMAGE0###
Problem 3
a.   Can you and your partner draw the same rectangle without looking at each other's drawing?
Partner A: Draw a rectangle on one of the grids provided. Describe it to your partner without telling them the total number of squares.
Partner B: Draw the rectangle your partner describes to you.
b.   Place your two rectangles next to each other. Discuss: What is the same? What is different?
c.   Switch roles and repeat.
###TABLE0###

Task
Materials
• Copies of a rectangle with edges marked (one for each student/ group, see attached blackline master)
• A straight edge tool
Actions
The teacher should guide students through these actions, as the text in this task is too complex for some second graders.
Draw a grid on the rectangle by connecting each mark to the one directly across from it on the opposite edge.
###IMAGE0###
The grid separates the rectangle into many little squares. How many squares are there?
There are five little squares in each row. Count by fives to find how many squares there are in the entire rectangle.
What other methods can you think of to quickly count how many squares there are in the entire rectangle?
Write a number in each little square to count them and show that your answers are correct.
One number sentence which shows the total number of squares is 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 15.  Write another number sentence which shows the total number of squares.

metadata.title: Partitioning a Rectangle into Unit Squares

problems.text:
Problem 1
a.   The tick marks on the sides of the rectangle below are 1 unit apart.
###IMAGE0###
What is the area in square units? Show or explain how you know.
b.   The top side of this rectangle is marked off in unit lengths. The left side is labeled with the length in units.
###IMAGE1###
What is the area in square units? Show or explain how you know.
Problem 2
a.   Write a multiplication expression that represents the area of the following figure. Then find its area.
###IMAGE2###
b.   Frederick draws another rectangle with the same area. What might the length and width of Frederick's rectangle be?
Problem 3
a.   The area of the rectangle below is 42 square units. Find the missing side length.
###IMAGE3###
b.   Explain how you can use either multiplication or division to solve Part (a).

Task
Materials
• Copies of a rectangle with edges marked (one for each student/ group, see attached blackline master)
• A straight edge tool
Actions
The teacher should guide students through these actions, as the text in this task is too complex for some second graders.
Draw a grid on the rectangle by connecting each mark to the one directly across from it on the opposite edge.
###IMAGE0###
The grid separates the rectangle into many little squares. How many squares are there?
There are five little squares in each row. Count by fives to find how many squares there are in the entire rectangle.
What other methods can you think of to quickly count how many squares there are in the entire rectangle?
Write a number in each little square to count them and show that your answers are correct.
One number sentence which shows the total number of squares is 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 = 15.  Write another number sentence which shows the total number of squares.

tasks.task_text:
2.G Partitioning a Rectangle into
Unit Squares
Task
Materials
• Copies of a rectangle with edges marked (one for each student/ group, see attached
blackline master)
• A straight edge tool
Actions
The teacher should guide students through these actions, as the text in this task is too
complex for some second graders.
a. Draw a grid on the rectangle by connecting each mark to the one directly across
from it on the opposite edge.
1
Illustrative 
Mathematics

b. The grid separates the rectangle into many little squares. How many squares are
there?
c. There are five little squares in each row. Count by fives to find how many squares
there are in the entire rectangle.  
d. What other methods can you think of to quickly count how many squares there are
in the entire rectangle?
e. Write a number in each little square to count them and show that your answers are
correct.
f. One number sentence which shows the total number of squares is 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 + 3 =
15.  Write another number sentence which shows the total number of squares.
